Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a strict routine. I'd study when I felt like it or when something was due soon. Sometimes I'd have these bursts where I'd do a ton of work, and then other times I'd go days without touching anything course-related. I'm more of a hands-on learner, so sitting down to study in a traditional way never came naturally to me.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Deadlines were basically my only real motivator to get things done. If I had extracurriculars or something more interesting going on, studying would get pushed back. I'd cram a lot into the days before something was due rather than spreading it out evenly.
Q3: Usually it was because I needed to check if something was due or to submit an assignment. Sometimes a friend from class would message me about something, and that would remind me to log in. It wasn't really a habit I developed on my own—more like external triggers pushed me to do it.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. There were maybe a week or two where I was logging in more regularly, but it never stuck. The LMS just felt like another thing I had to do rather than something that fit naturally into my day. My peer support group helped remind me sometimes, which was nice.
Q5: The videos were actually pretty helpful when I watched them because I could see things being done rather than just reading about them. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so anything visual or hands-on clicked better for me. Discussion posts were okay too because hearing other students' perspectives helped me understand things I'd missed.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They just felt like walls of text, and I'd lose focus quickly. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, I probably skipped it. I know that probably hurt my exam scores, but I just couldn't get myself to engage with that stuff.
Q7: If I could see how it connected to something real or practical, I'd actually want to do it. Activities that felt like busy work or just checking boxes didn't motivate me at all. When I could work with my hands or collaborate with someone, that's when I felt like my time was well spent.
Q8: Optional stuff basically didn't exist for me. If it wasn't required, I probably didn't do it. Required activities at least had a deadline attached, so there was some pressure to complete them. Optional activities felt like extra stress I didn't need, even though I know some of them might have helped.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download slides or materials and then study from my phone or laptop without being connected to the LMS. Sometimes I'd work through problems with friends in person, which helped me way more than staring at a screen alone. That kind of hands-on collaboration was my real study method.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was genuinely learning but it wouldn't show up anywhere online. Like when I'd meet up with classmates and we'd work through practice problems together on a whiteboard. Or when I'd rewatch downloaded videos on my commute. The LMS wouldn't capture any of that.
Q11: All the time. I'd open a video or document once, download it, and then use it offline for days. It was easier to study on my own terms without needing internet or being logged in. So my one view didn't really reflect how many times I actually looked at the material.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really tough. I had extracurriculars ramping up, and I was feeling pretty stressed and anxious about everything piling up. My motivation just tanked, and even logging into the LMS felt like a chore. The peer support I got helped pull me back a bit, but those weeks were rough.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or stressed, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Competing responsibilities like my extracurriculars and just life stuff made it hard to prioritize coursework. I'd end up doing the bare minimum online even if I was still trying to learn in other ways.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be logged in, technically engaging, but not absorbing anything. It was like going through the motions without actually being present mentally.
Q15: I put off discussion posts a lot because they felt awkward and time-consuming. Even though I knew participating mattered, I'd wait until the last minute or barely meet the requirement. Same with longer readings—I knew they were important but kept telling myself I'd get to them later.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire system. I'd check the LMS mainly to see when things were due and then work backward from there. Without a deadline, I probably wouldn't do something. It's not great, but that pressure was the only thing that consistently got me moving.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did make me want to try a bit harder on the next thing. Positive comments especially gave me a little boost. But if feedback took too long to come back, I'd already have moved on mentally and it didn't affect me as much.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work at my own pace, but bad because I need more structure to stay on track. Without regular check-ins or required milestones, I'd drift and fall behind.
Q19: Having everything online made it easy to procrastinate because there was no physical class to show up to. But the video content was helpful since I could pause and rewatch. More interactive or hands-on activities would have kept me way more engaged than just reading and watching.
Q20: I think some students might be searching around, trying to figure things out, but getting stuck or confused. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity. They could be lost in the system or just clicking around without really knowing what to submit or how.
Q21: That kind of describes me, actually. My LMS activity was low, but I still passed because I did a lot of studying offline and with friends. The system doesn't see the group study sessions or the times I reviewed downloaded materials. Low login numbers don't mean someone isn't learning.
Q22: Logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually understood anything or if you were mentally present. They miss all the offline work, the conversations with classmates, the real moments of learning that happen away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort, not just going through the motions. It's about being curious and wanting to understand, not just checking boxes to pass. For me, engagement happens more when I'm doing something hands-on or talking with others.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my peer support group helped me stay afloat or how my extracurriculars sometimes energized me to do better. It also wouldn't capture my offline studying or the fact that I'm actually in a decent mood about the course overall, even if my numbers look mediocre.
